title: Constituency Parsing
concept: Constituency parsing breaks a sentence into nested phrases (Noun Phrase, Verb Phrase), showing how parts group together hierarchically.

exercises.prompt: When you read, do you process sentences word-by-word, or do you naturally group thematically, or do you group them? Or do you naturally, or in:

options.text: Groups of words that belong together (phrases)
Only the first and last words of a sentence

exercises.explain: Just like we group numbers in math using parentheses, sentences have structural groups of words that belong together, known as phrases.

cards.title: Grouping Words Naturally
cards.body: How do our brains make sense of a long sentence? We don't just read it as a flat list of words. We instantly group words into meaningful chunks. Computers use Constituency Parsing to do the exact same thing!

cards.title: What is Constituency Parsing?
cards.body: Constituency Parsing is a way of analyzing a sentence by breaking it down into nested sub-phrases. It shows how words combine to form a Noun Phrase (NP) or a Verb Phrase (VP), building a tree structure all the way up to the full Sentence (S).

cards.title: Nesting Dolls
cards.body: Think of a sentence like a set of Russian nesting dolls. You open the biggest doll (the whole Sentence), inside you find a medium doll (the Noun Phrase), and inside that, you find smaller dolls (individual words like "The" and "dog"). Every phrase fits snugly inside a larger one!

cards.title: Parsing "The happy puppy"
cards.body: Let's look at the sentence "The happy puppy barked." A constituency parser groups "The happy puppy" into a Noun Phrase (NP) and "barked" into a Verb Phrase (VP). Together, they form the complete Sentence (S).

cards.title: Not Just Word Links
cards.body: People often think constituency parsing just links words one-by-one. Actually, it groups words into structural blocks (constituents). It cares about how phrases nest inside other phrases, not just individual word connections.

cards.title: Parsing Styles
cards.body: Constituency parsing looks at how words group into phrases, whereas dependency parsing looks at direct relationships between individual words.

left.text: Groups words into nested phrase blocks: [The blue bird] [flew away].

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of Constituency Parsing?

options.text: It groups words into nested phrases (Noun Phrases, Verb Phrases) to show sentence structure.
It only counts the number of nouns and verbs in a text.
It translates English sentences directly into computer code.
It randomly splits a sentence into two equal halves.

exercises.explain: Constituency parsing breaks a sentence down into hierarchical, nested phrase structures (like NP and VP) that form a tree.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Constituency parsing represents a sentence as a tree structure of nested phrases.
exercises.explain: Yes! Because phrases nest inside other phrases, the final output looks like a family tree, starting from the whole sentence at the top down to individual words at the bottom.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase type for the words "The smart student".
exercises.sentence: In the sentence "The smart student read a book", the words "The smart student" form a [blank].
exercises.explain: In constituency parsing, "The smart student" is grouped together as a Noun Phrase (NP) before combining with the verb.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs represents a Constituency Parse?

options.text: A tree diagram showing "The cat" grouped into a Noun Phrase (NP).
An arrow pointing directly from the verb "chased" to the noun "mouse".
A simple list labeling "cat" as a NOUN and "chased" as a VERB.

exercises.explain: Constituency parsing shows nested phrase groups (like Noun Phrases). Sentence 1 shows this grouping, whereas Sentence 2 shows direct word-to-word dependencies (arrows), and Sentence 3 is just part-of-speech tagging.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a grammar-checking robot. Why would you choose to use a Constituency Parser instead of just looking at individual words?

options.text: It connects words directly with arrows showing who does what to whom.
It groups words into nested phrases (like Noun Phrases) that build up to a full sentence.
It translates sentences word-for-word into another language.
It counts the number of letters in each word of a sentence.

exercises.explain: A constituency parser groups words into phrases (like Noun Phrase and Verb Phrase) before grouping them into a complete Sentence, showing the hierarchical, nested structure of grammar.
